title: Understanding Variance of Estimator
text: We are now finding the variance of the estimator \alpha_hat, not its bias. Variance tells us how much the estimator changes from sample to sample. A smaller variance means the estimator is more stable.

title: Variance Rule for Constants
text: If c is a constant, then $$Var(cX)=c^2 \operatorname{Var}\left(X\right)$$. Using this: Var(\alpha_hat)=Var((1/100)*sum{i\=1}{100}{X_i})=(1/100)**2*Var(sum{i\=1}{100}{X_i})

title: Independence in Context of Variance
text: Because the $$X_i$$ are independent, their covariance is $$0$$. So, the variance of their sum is simply the sum of their variances. Using this: (1/100)**2*Var(sum{i\=1}{100}{X_i})=(1/100)**2*(Var(X_1)+Var(X_2)+...+Var(X_100))

title: Use the Common Variance Value
text: The problem says each observation has the same variance of $${\sigma}^2$$. So, the sum of variances becomes $$100$$ copies of $${\sigma}^2$$.

title: Simplify the Sum
text: Simplifying: $$\left(\frac{1}{100}\right)^2 \left(\operatorname{Var}\left(X_1\right)+\operatorname{Var}\left(X_2\right)+...+\operatorname{Var}\left(X_{100}\right)\right)=\left(\frac{1}{100}\right)^2 100{\sigma}^2$$ What is the simplified expression?

title: Understanding Variance Conceptually
text: This shows that averaging $$100$$ independent observations reduces variability by a factor of $$100$$. Var(\alpha_hat) describes how much the sample mean would vary across repeated random samples of $$100$$ students. Even if individual students vary a lot, averaging many students makes the estimator more stable.
